content:
# Luke Chapter 17 — Translation Disagreements

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translators:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse-by-Verse Disagreements

### 17:1 — Rendering of ΑΝΕΝΔΕΚΤΟΝ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΤΟΥ ΤΑ ΣΚΑΝΔΑΛΑ ΜΗ ΕΛΘΕΙΝ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "It is impossible [for] the stumbling-blocks not to come" |
| LOGOS | "It is impossible that stumbling-blocks should not come" |
| PNEUMA | "It is inevitable that things will come that cause people to stumble into sin" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"It is impossible that stumbling-blocks should not come"** |
| Rationale | LOGOS's subjunctive construction renders the articular infinitive naturally. PNEUMA paraphrases too freely, adding "into sin" and replacing the noun "stumbling-blocks" with a clause. |

### 17:2 — "thrown" vs. "cast"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "thrown into the sea" |
| LOGOS | "cast into the sea" |
| PNEUMA | "thrown into the sea" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"thrown into the sea"** |
| Rationale | 2-to-1 majority. "Thrown" is more natural; "cast" is slightly archaic. |

### 17:3 — "Take heed" vs. "Watch yourselves"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Take-heed to yourselves" |
| LOGOS | "Take heed to yourselves" |
| PNEUMA | "Watch yourselves carefully" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Take heed to yourselves"** |
| Rationale | EMET and LOGOS agree on the phrase; PNEUMA adds "carefully" not present in the Greek. |

### 17:6 — "mustard seed" vs. "mustard"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a grain of mustard" |
| LOGOS | "a grain of mustard seed" |
| PNEUMA | "even as small as a mustard seed" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"a grain of mustard seed"** |
| Rationale | "Mustard seed" is the standard English idiom for ΚΟΚΚΟΝ ΣΙΝΑΠΕΩΣ. EMET's "grain of mustard" is literal but less idiomatic. |

### 17:7 — "servant" vs. "slave"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "slave" |
| LOGOS | "servant" |
| PNEUMA | "servant" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"servant"** |
| Rationale | 2-to-1 majority. Consistent with SOPHIA's practice for ΔΟΥΛΟΣ in parabolic contexts. |

### 17:7 — "tending sheep" vs. "shepherding"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "shepherding" |
| LOGOS | "tending sheep" |
| PNEUMA | "tending sheep" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"tending sheep"** |
| Rationale | 2-to-1 majority. "Tending sheep" is more accessible while preserving the meaning of ΠΟΙΜΑΙΝΟΝΤΑ. |

### 17:8 — "gird yourself" vs. "put on your apron"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "having girded-yourself" |
| LOGOS | "gird yourself" |
| PNEUMA | "put on your apron" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"gird yourself"** |
| Rationale | LOGOS preserves the formal register of ΠΕΡΙΖΩΣΑΜΕΝΟΣ. PNEUMA's "apron" is interpretive. |

### 17:10 — "unprofitable" vs. "unworthy"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "unprofitable slaves" |
| LOGOS | "unprofitable servants" |
| PNEUMA | "unworthy servants" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"unprofitable servants"** |
| Rationale | "Unprofitable" precisely renders ΑΧΡΕΙΟΙ (from Α + ΧΡΕΙΑ, "without use/profit"). PNEUMA's "unworthy" adds a moral dimension absent from the Greek. |

### 17:13 — "their voices" (plural) vs. "their voice" (singular)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "their voice" (singular, preserving ΦΩΝΗΝ) |
| LOGOS | "their voices" (English plural) |
| PNEUMA | "in loud voices" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"their voices"** |
| Rationale | English idiom naturally uses the plural for multiple speakers. EMET's singular, while textually precise, reads unnaturally. |

### 17:14 — "Go" vs. "Having gone"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Having gone, show yourselves" |
| LOGOS | "Go, show yourselves" |
| PNEUMA | "Go and show yourselves" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Go, show yourselves"** |
| Rationale | "Go, show yourselves" renders ΠΟΡΕΥΘΕΝΤΕΣ ΕΠΙΔΕΙΞΑΤΕ naturally. EMET's participial form is over-literal. |

### 17:17 — "But where are the nine?" vs. "But the nine — where [are they]?"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But the nine — where [are they]?" |
| LOGOS | "But where are the nine?" |
| PNEUMA | "Where are the other nine?" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"But where are the nine?"** |
| Rationale | Natural English word order. EMET mimics Greek word order unnecessarily. PNEUMA adds "other" not in the Greek. |

### 17:19 — "has saved you" vs. "has made you well"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "has saved you" |
| LOGOS | "has saved you" |
| PNEUMA | "has made you well" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"has saved you"** |
| Rationale | 2-to-1 majority. "Saved" preserves the full theological weight of ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ. PNEUMA's "made you well" reduces ΣΩΖΩ to physical healing, which all three translators note in their commentary is inadequate here. |

### 17:21 — "in the midst of you" vs. "within you" vs. "among you"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "within you" |
| LOGOS | "in the midst of you" |
| PNEUMA | "among you" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"in the midst of you"** |
| Rationale | Significant theological disagreement. EMET reads ΕΝΤΟΣ ΥΜΩΝ as interior ("within"), LOGOS as spatial ("in the midst"), PNEUMA as communal ("among"). All three note in commentary that the interior reading is less probable given the Pharisaic audience. LOGOS's "in the midst of you" best preserves the ambiguity while favoring the spatial-presence reading. |

### 17:25 — "he must suffer many things" vs. "he must suffer terribly"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "it is necessary [for] him to suffer many [things]" |
| LOGOS | "he must suffer many things" |
| PNEUMA | "he must suffer terribly" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he must suffer many things"** |
| Rationale | PNEUMA replaces ΠΟΛΛΑ ("many things") with the interpretive adverb "terribly." LOGOS renders the Greek precisely. |

### 17:29 — "brimstone" vs. "sulfur"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "brimstone" |
| LOGOS | "brimstone" |
| PNEUMA | "sulfur" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"brimstone"** |
| Rationale | 2-to-1 majority. "Brimstone" is the traditional biblical rendering of ΘΕΙΟΝ and maintains the formal register. |

### 17:33 — "life" vs. "soul" for ΨΥΧΗ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "his soul" |
| LOGOS | "his life" |
| PNEUMA | "his life" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"his life"** |
| Rationale | 2-to-1 majority. "Life" renders ΨΥΧΗ in this context more accurately. "Soul" introduces a body-soul dualism not intended by the text. |

### 17:33 — "shall preserve it alive" vs. "will bring-it-to-life"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "will bring-it-to-life" |
| LOGOS | "shall preserve it alive" |
| PNEUMA | "will keep it alive" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"shall preserve it alive"** |
| Rationale | LOGOS captures both the preservation and the life-giving aspects of ΖΩΟΓΟΝΗΣΕΙ. EMET's paraphrase is acceptable; PNEUMA's is simplified. |

### 17:34 — "in that night" vs. "in this night"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in this night" |
| LOGOS | "in that night" |
| PNEUMA | "on that night" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"in that night"** |
| Rationale | Though ΤΑΥΤΗ can mean "this," in the eschatological context Jesus speaks of a future event. "That" is the appropriate English deictic. |

### 17:37 — "eagles" vs. "vultures"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "eagles" |
| LOGOS | "eagles" |
| PNEUMA | "vultures" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"eagles"** |
| Rationale | 2-to-1 majority. "Eagles" is the literal rendering of ΑΕΤΟΙ. All translators note that vultures are the more likely zoological referent, but this belongs in commentary rather than translation. |

---

## Summary Statistics

| Category | Count |
|---|---|
| Total verses | 37 (36 translated + 1 absent from Sinaiticus) |
| Verses with disagreements | 19 |
| Verses with full agreement | 18 |
| SOPHIA followed LOGOS | 18 |
| SOPHIA followed EMET | 3 |
| SOPHIA followed PNEUMA | 0 |
| SOPHIA followed EMET+LOGOS majority | 5 |
| SOPHIA followed LOGOS+PNEUMA majority | 7 |
| SOPHIA followed EMET+PNEUMA majority | 2 |
| Significant theological disagreements | 1 (17:21 — ΕΝΤΟΣ ΥΜΩΝ) |

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA, arbiter of the council of EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA.*
